title: DAY 6 of the War | An Oasis of Peace in the Chaos
text: This place feels seriously unreal. Especially since I'm just about the only person around. Hey guys, how's it going? So, today is Thursday, March 5th, 2026. Currently 84° Fahrenheit out there. That is 29° C. This is day six since the war began. So, uh I am going to start off here by giving a very very quick recap of the overall situation for those who might not have, you know, seen past videos. I will make it nice and quick, a minute or two, since I know that my past videos have been a lot of talking, explaining stuff. I'm sure that many of you understand, but just to kind of give a little added context here as to, for example, why I told the story of my escape from Qatar to UAE here rather than filming the whole thing. I think it's fairly obvious, but uh number one, I needed to be focused on the trip and not be distracted by filming. Number two, because I don't want to get any um unwanted attention or uh make anybody suspicious or annoyed or whatever. Number three, you can't film at the border crossings, of course, and that was the kind of most exciting part there. Number four, most of the journey was at night and there was really nothing to show. Anyways, number five, Muhammad didn't want to be in the video and understandably so. So, uh anyways, now a quick synopsis of uh just what has happened in the past week. So, I had a flight booked from Europe to Mumbai, India with a layover in Doha, Qatar on the day of the attacks on February 28th. So, that was the day of my layover and so it was just really bad timing that I landed early in the morning and then had a flight out in the evening to India, but the attacks occurred and so airport closed and no flights going out. And so, I was there in Doha for several days and then I got an amazing offer from a viewer of mine who lives in Abu Dhabi, who said, "I'd be willing to go pick you up." And I said, you know, "Yes, I'll do it." And so, uh I left Doha a couple of days ago. Let's see, yeah, that would have been like 2 days ago. And he left driving towards me. We met on the other side of the Saudi Arabia-Doha border and met up, really good guy, and he got me all the way here to Al Ain. I've been to Al Ain before, back in 2017, and so I had chosen this as a place to get to to then figure out my other plans because it is a medium-sized city, not a target like the other major cities of Dubai and Abu Dhabi here in UAE, which are in fact getting hit by missiles, or at least they're targeted. There have been attacks that have been successful at least in Dubai. I'm not sure about Abu Dhabi, but the missiles are flying in this direction. I haven't heard any here in Al Ain today. So, this is a good place to be, but of course, I want to ultimately get out of the region because things are escalating fast and looking like they are not going to be finishing up with this attack as far as the US and Israel attacking Iran. That is not going to be over anytime soon whatsoever. And so, things could very well just get a lot worse. As of now, airports have opened up, not in Doha as far as I know, but the projection is tomorrow, the 6th, that airport could open up. So, perhaps if I'd stayed there, I could have caught a flight out of Doha, but once the airport opens, who knows when you can actually get a flight out anyways. And so, I have a plan in place that I am very cautiously optimistic about. I will just give the general sense. Obviously, I want to be careful about specifics, but uh I have three flights booked out of Muscat Airport on the same day going to three different locations using three different airlines. And so, that increases my likelihood of getting out a fair amount as long as the Muscat Airport is in fact open, which the latest info is that it is actually open and flights are going out, perhaps limited. And so, I booked these flights just on expedia.com, but there's absolutely no guarantee, of course, that they are going to fly out. But, uh three flights increases my chances, and so that is the hope. If you happen to be curious, well, how much money am I spending on all of these flights? They actually weren't too expensive. So, all three added up comes to about $900 US. I am getting refunds on these flights, the ones that I had booked in Doha, flying out of uh Doha, Qatar. The refunds are now coming back. I realize that is not the most important thing at this point, but I had made a whole lot of flights, and so it is reassuring that I am starting to get the refunds coming back on those. All three of the flights that I have booked out of Muscat have 24-hour cancellation, and then also if the flights are canceled by the airlines, then I should get refunds on those as well. That is the overall general situation as it is now. As you can see, a uh nice day here in Al Ain, and I am staying here again tonight, and then will make the uh journey to Oman tomorrow. I'm not quite sure how that is going to happen. I tried to book a bus yesterday at the bus station. You actually need to use uh the website. I tried the website. I'm not able to find the uh direct bus being available from here all the way to Muscat, and so uh I'm not sure how that is going to happen, but it is about a 3 and 1/2 hour drive from Al Ain here to Muscat, Oman. So, fully doable within a day. The question is how to make it happen if that direct bus isn't available. Probably it's full up. So, uh I will be working on that over the course of today. But, for now, I want to get out of the room in part just to get out of the room and go do a little something for an hour or two or whatever. And so, for a little something more than just hanging out talking in the room, then let's get out there and show a little bit of AlAin. This is a really nice city. So, this is AlAin. Yesterday after uh I finished filming the video telling the whole story of getting here, which was that was in the Oasis back in there. Really, really pretty uh like park with palm trees and uh like these walkways and stuff. Really cool. But, I won't uh go in there now since I made a whole video showing that. But, uh after that, I popped in there and got some drinks and snacks and stuff. Really uh nice uh market with tons of stuff. And now I am going to walk over to the bus station. Even though I think that I'm out of luck getting a bus tomorrow, but uh there's a market there. So, wanted to uh show the market a little bit. And then uh I have some other places in mind to check out. And I got some information from the front desk about uh how to cross the border tomorrow without the bus. So, here you can see this really nice uh scene which must be somewhere here in AlAin. Maybe back in the Oasis area. It's a big area. Or else elsewhere. Obviously, it's next to a fort, which I'm thinking of uh visiting. And look at the uh amazing apartments or whatever. Or maybe this is something that is slated to be constructed, but anyways, it is a very, very uh pleasant city. But then here you can see this is on the edge of the oasis. So all the palm trees out there and then here you have all these uh buildings. I'm not exactly sure what they are. If they're shops, cafes, or little apartments or something. And then look at this. All these electric uh car charging spots. So this city is really kind of cutting edge. And they intentionally decided not to go the way of Dubai and Abu Dhabi with all the big skyscrapers. And so there's a limit on how high the buildings can be. Like maybe that mosque minaret out there could be the tallest building. Definitely the tallest one I'm seeing from here. And so yesterday I started my video walking over there into the uh oasis. And so it's free. You just walk in. And then I think that I came out over here. And so you can walk in at various points and then just kind of get lost in there. And there's a lot more to see than what I showed, I guess. So it's a really uh just very pleasant city in which they're planning it and constructing things in a really just nice uh way. >> All right, so I decided to just catch a taxi out to the Jali Park as you can see and the fort is here. About a just like 6-7 minute taxi ride there. It was about four bucks. So very very reasonable and very pleasant here. Saw an interesting-looking monument over here. Definitely looking very very quiet for a variety of reasons. Number one, of course, there's a war on. I don't know how much that is necessarily impacting things here. You can see plenty of traffic. I forget, but on that subject, another reason why it is quiet is it is Ramadan. Of course, the uh Muslim month of fasting and prayer. And up there, I can see a fort. And then look at this. But, uh too often as the Seinfeld series began, the first episode then, uh it's Jerry remarking to George saying "That button is in the exact wrong spot." So, here it's like a little too much, you know, chest going on. And then, often the uh button is up here and then you do that and it's too tight. And so, like with one button you go from like disco chest hair to like schoolboy, like two buttoned up. Anyway, let's get into the fort. Al Jahili Fort looks like it's open. I checked online, it said it was open until 7:00 p.m. The north gate forms part of the original late 19th century enclosure built around 1897 by Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, Zayed the First. The original form of the gate can be seen in an archive photo taken by explorer Wilfred Thesiger around 1948. During the 1950s, the original arch gate was either widened deliberately or knocked down for access by 3-ton military trucks. Okay. I love these kinds of uh mud forts like this. Just love the style. So, looks like there has been or is going to be a uh Certainly in the evening. The fort complex at Al Jahili contains within one site many different elements of traditional mud brick architecture. These include a round watchtower, old fort with defensive towers, two large walled enclosures, and a mosque. The oldest parts are the inner fort and the separate multi-tiered round tower. Getting back to the 1890s. Will I see anybody else other than some mannequins? Love that fort entrance. This honestly feels a bit odd to be in here and there's absolutely nobody. No person manning the gate or anything. But I just got a whiff of some really amazing smelling incense, I guess. Just for a second there. Traditional board games. Huh. I decided to stop in there again and ask one of the people at the desk about the bus tomorrow to Muscat because there is a bus once a day direct going over the border into Oman and straight to Muscat. That is by far the ideal situation to get over there in which they take you the whole way through the border and then it's only going to cost whatever, 20, 30, 40 bucks or something. Otherwise, it gets a lot more complicated, but uh unfortunately, I might have to take the complicated route because uh he looked at the website and uh I think maybe he didn't even understand how it works. I've been trying to book a ticket there. And so, he then said, "Well, just come by tomorrow at 1:00 and then you'll find out if you can get a seat." But, uh that is not a good idea. If it was at 8:00 in the morning, then I could do that. And then if I don't get a seat, then I get going the other way. But, 1:00 in the afternoon is getting late to realize that I need to start going the other way. Especially when it's a 3 and 1/2 hour drive to Muscat and plus border crossing. And if I'm not going to be on a direct bus, then it's definitely going to take longer. I won't get into trying to explain those options now, but uh that isn't going to work coming over here at 1:00.
